one_line_takeaway: By poisoning a RAG knowledge base served through LangChain/ChatChat and hiding harmful content behind keyword triggers plus Morse/Base64 obfuscation in PDF files, PLC turns retrieval into an indirect jailbreak channel that beats direct prompting by a wide margin (up to ~88% aggregate ASR) and, surprisingly, works best on the strongest models.
key_finding: PLC achieves high indirect-jailbreak success while direct jailbreaks with the same prompts are largely blocked. Aggregate ASR is 88.56% (dangerous behavior), 79.04% (chemical misuse), and 82.69% (discrimination), versus direct-jailbreak baselines of only 15.39%, 12.33%, and 6.03% respectively. Counterintuitively, stronger commercial models are MORE vulnerable to PLC: Xinghuo-3.5 reaches 98.5% on dangerous behavior while the weaker llama2-7b reaches only 71.5%, which the authors attribute to stronger models being better at decoding the Morse/Base64 obfuscation and following the injected instructions, whereas low-logic models fail to decode them (and longer prompts induce hallucination). Notably llama2-7b is the outlier in the reverse direction for DIRECT jailbreaks, where it is the most susceptible (e.g., 57.14% on chemicals, 40.38% on dangerous behavior) while commercial models are near zero.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Poisoned-LangChain (PLC), an indirect jailbreak attack against LLMs that weaponizes the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline instead of crafting a direct adversarial prompt. Rather than sending a malicious prompt to the model, the attacker plants a poisoned external knowledge base that is served to the model through a LangChain/ChatChat RAG stack; benign-looking user queries containing planted keyword triggers cause the retriever to inject disguised harmful content, which the LLM then expands into policy-violating output. The authors frame this as the 'first work to propose the concept of indirect jailbreak' via LangChain, and their own contribution is the PLC framework (trigger design + toxic knowledge-base construction + retrieval-time evasion), distinct from the direct-prompt jailbreak methods (AutoDAN, DAN, MASTERKEY, Tree-of-Attacks, etc.) they cite only in Related Work.
threat_model: Attacker controls (or can inject into) the external knowledge base that a RAG-enabled LLM application retrieves from. The paper's realized setting is direct interaction with a malicious knowledge base the attacker uploads into a LangChain-Chatchat deployment (SSH enabled, attacks issued via the web interface to mimic real deployments). The victim is any user querying the RAG chatbot: from the user's perspective the trigger and retrieval are subtle and imperceptible, so a benign-seeming question returns malicious guidance (e.g., the paper's example where the model advises filling a room with carbon monoxide). The authors explicitly note the current attack still requires direct access to the malicious knowledge base, and position remote poisoning of otherwise-benign knowledge bases as future work.
limitations: Self-described as 'preliminary experiments.' (1) Requires the attacker to directly upload/control the malicious knowledge base; remote poisoning of benign KBs is only future work, so the demonstrated threat is narrower than a fully remote supply-chain attack. (2) Small, hand-built evaluation: only 3 categories x 10 items = 30 attack contents, all self-constructed, with no standard jailbreak benchmark used. (3) ASR judging procedure is not specified (no stated human-eval protocol or automated judge), so success criteria and inter-rater reliability are unclear. (4) Scope limited to six Chinese LLMs (plus llama2-7b); no GPT-4/Claude-class Western frontier models tested. (5) No defense is proposed or evaluated beyond noting the PDF-vs-txt embedding-filter gap; mitigation is left to future work. (6) Temperature fixed at 1.0 adds output randomness that is not ablated. (7) The evasion trick (PDF converting to bypass LangChain's txt keyword filter) is tied to a specific tool version and may not generalize.

datasets_used: Self-constructed poisoned knowledge base sourced from Wikipedia and search engines, filtered via a censor-capable LLM repeat test
Three harm categories x 10 hand-crafted jailbreak items each (30 total): incitement of dangerous behavior, misuse of chemicals, illegal/discriminatory actions
LangChain-Chatchat (ChatChat) used as the RAG host framework
quantitative_results: Aggregate PLC ASR: 88.56% (dangerous behavior), 79.04% (misuse of chemicals), 82.69% (illegal/discriminatory)
Aggregate direct-jailbreak baseline ASR: 15.39%, 12.33%, 6.03% for the same three categories
PLC per-model peak: Xinghuo-3.5 98.50% on dangerous behavior; ChatGlm3-6B 97.00%; Qwen-14B-Chat 96.00%
Direct jailbreak on commercial models near zero (e.g., ChatGlm3-6B 1.49%/0.00%/1.50%; Xinghuo-3.5 3.96%/0.00%/0.00%; Qwen 19.50%/0.19%/0.00%), while llama2-7b is highest at 40.38%/57.14%/22.77%
All models run at temperature 1.0; ASR defined as n/m
baselines_compared: Direct (non-RAG) jailbreak attack using the same prompts and hyperparameters (Table 3) as the primary comparison
Six target LLMs: ChatGlm2-6B (Zhipu), ChatGlm3-6B (Zhipu), Xinghuo-3.5 (iFlytek, API), Qwen-14B-Chat (Alibaba, API), Ernie-3.5 (Baidu, API), llama2-7B (Meta)
